prompt: Photograph taken by the James Webb Space Telescope of an unknown region of deep space. 16:9 aspect ratio. The image is almost entirely black — the cold vacuum of intergalactic void. In the center-left third of the frame, there is a massive structure that defies physics: a vertical fissure in spacetime, approximately 200 light-years tall, where the fabric of the universe has split open. The edges of the fissure glow with faint bioluminescent gold — not bright, not neon, but the color of molten bronze cooling in vacuum (#8B7340 to #C9A84C gradient). The light traces the fracture like veins in obsidian rock. Through the opening, an entirely different universe is faintly visible — not colorful, but shifted: reversed star patterns, impossible gravitational lensing bending background galaxies into arcs, a sense of depth that goes infinitely inward. The surrounding space contains real astronomical phenomena: extremely distant galaxy clusters rendered as tiny smudges of warm light, individual foreground stars with authentic diffraction spikes from the JWST mirror segments (6-pointed, asymmetric), and wispy traces of dark molecular cloud silhouetted against faint background emission. One thin ribbon of ionized hydrogen stretches across the upper portion — barely visible, dark crimson (#1A0808), like a wound in space. The entire color palette is: 90% pure black and near-black, 8% deep warm tones (burgundy, umber, dark gold), 2% faint point-source whites. This looks like an actual NASA press release image — clinical, scientific, unsettling. Shot with NIRCam and MIRI instruments, composite infrared wavelengths mapped to visible spectrum. Absolute pixel-level sharpness. No artistic interpretation. No fantasy. No illustration. This is raw data from a telescope pointed at something that changed our understanding of cosmology. The image should feel lonely, ancient, and vast beyond comprehension.
